one_line_takeaway: QLoRA-distilled chain-of-thought self-reflection on Llama-Guard-3-8B lifts adversarial-jailbreak recall from 51.3% to 92.1% on WildGuardTest and cuts JailbreakBench attack success rate from 10.3% to 1.8%, but the paper's own ablation shows SFT alone (no reflection text) captures most of the aggregate F1 gain, with reflection specifically responsible for the targeted adversarial-recall boost at a cost in precision.
key_finding: On WildGuardTest (n=1,699), Reflect-Guard improves F1 from 0.770 to 0.842 (+7.2pp), driven by recall rising from 0.663 to 0.863 (+20.0pp) at the cost of precision falling from 0.919 to 0.821 (-9.8pp). Gains concentrate on the adversarial subset (n=796): recall rises from 0.513 to 0.921 (+40.8pp), F1 from 0.639 to 0.802. On JailbreakBench (n=282), overall attack success rate drops from 10.3% to 1.8% (82.5% relative reduction), with per-method detection rates improving from 93.0%->99.0% (GCG), 98.0%->100.0% (JBC), and 75.6%->95.1% (PAIR). The ablation study is the paper's most important internal finding: prompting alone without fine-tuning (Condition A) yields no benefit over the clean baseline (F1 0.761 vs 0.770), showing SFT is necessary; SFT on verdict labels alone (Condition B, no reflection text) already recovers most of the aggregate F1 gain (0.860 vs Reflect-Guard's 0.842) and achieves 100% JailbreakBench detection, actually higher than the full method's 98.2%. Reflection training (Condition D vs B) trades 1.8pp of overall F1 for a targeted +7.6pp gain in adversarial-subset recall (0.921 vs 0.845), with an asymmetric precision/recall pattern across adversarial vs non-adversarial subsets that the authors argue rules out a simple global-threshold-shift explanation. Ground-truth-informed teacher reflections (Condition D) beat label-blind reflections (Condition C) by only 0.6pp F1 (0.842 vs 0.836).
core_contribution: Reflect-Guard: a method that augments the Llama-Guard-3-8B safety classifier with an explicit chain-of-thought self-reflection step, trained via QLoRA on reflection annotations distilled from GPT-4o-mini. The model is fine-tuned to first generate a short <reflection> analysis of a prompt's adversarial framing and intent, then emit a safe/unsafe verdict, using only 1,000 training examples (500 from WildGuardMix, 500 from AdvBench) and ~42M trainable LoRA parameters (0.5% of the 8B base model). No external teacher API is needed at inference time; the reflection capability is fully internalized in the LoRA adapter.
threat_model: Defends an LLM input-safety classifier against adversarial jailbreak prompts that disguise harmful intent through role-play, fictional framing, indirect/educational requests, and gradient-optimized adversarial suffixes (e.g., GCG). The paper treats the baseline Llama-Guard-3-8B as the defended system and evaluates its vulnerability to prompts drawn from WildGuardTest's adversarial subset and JailbreakBench (GCG, JBC template-based, and PAIR semantic jailbreaks against Vicuna-13B-v1.5). It does not model attacks against the reflection mechanism itself (e.g., prompts crafted to game the reflection step), which the paper flags as an open risk (reflection can be 'deceived by sufficiently sophisticated framings').
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) single training run with no multi-seed variance reported (they estimate +/-1-2 F1pp based on comparable QLoRA work); (2) training set of only 1,000 examples may not cover the full diversity of adversarial techniques -- 78 harmful examples are missed by all five ablation conditions, concentrated in private-information and social-stereotype categories underrepresented in training; (3) teacher bias transfer risk -- reflections are distilled from GPT-4o-mini with access to ground-truth labels, risking idiosyncratic phrasing or transfer of GPT-4o-mini's own safety-judgment biases (partially, not fully, mitigated by Condition C); (4) Reflect-Guard introduces 108 new false positives versus baseline (142 total vs 34), concentrated on benign prompts with adversarial-looking surface structure (e.g., a DEF CON cybersecurity-competition narrative flagged as harmful), which could burden legitimate security-research users; (5) no multilingual evaluation -- both training data and teacher reflections are English-only, and the authors discuss (without testing) three concrete cross-lingual failure risks: reflection capability not transferring to non-English inputs, cultural specificity of what counts as "harm," and cross-lingual adversarial techniques (code-switching, transliteration) unseen in training. The paper is also candid that most of the aggregate F1 gain traces to SFT on the decision boundary rather than reflection reasoning per se (Condition B nearly matches or exceeds Condition D on several metrics), so the 'reflection' framing should be read as a targeted adversarial-recall mechanism layered on top of an SFT gain, not the sole driver of the headline numbers.

datasets_used: WildGuardMix (training split, 500 of 1,000 fine-tuning examples; test split WildGuardTest, n=1,699, used for evaluation)
JailbreakBench (n=282 evaluation prompts: 100 GCG, 100 JBC, 82 PAIR, generated against Vicuna-13B-v1.5)
quantitative_results: WildGuardTest overall: F1 0.770 -> 0.842 (+7.2pp), Accuracy 0.825 -> 0.856, Precision 0.919 -> 0.821 (-9.8pp), Recall 0.663 -> 0.863 (+20.0pp)
WildGuardTest adversarial subset (n=796): Recall 0.513 -> 0.921 (+40.8pp), F1 0.639 -> 0.802, Accuracy 0.751 -> 0.805, Precision 0.845 -> 0.710
WildGuardTest non-adversarial subset (n=903): F1 0.867 -> 0.882, Precision 0.964 -> 0.960, Recall 0.787 -> 0.816
JailbreakBench overall: ASR 10.3% -> 1.8% (82.5% relative reduction); detection rate 89.7% -> 98.2%
JailbreakBench by method: GCG 93.0% -> 99.0%; JBC 98.0% -> 100.0%; PAIR 75.6% -> 95.1%
False positives: baseline 34 vs Reflect-Guard 142 (108 new FPs, all on adversarial-subset benign prompts)
False negatives: baseline 291 vs Reflect-Guard 103 (32 private information, 26 social stereotypes/discrimination, 16 other)
Ablation Condition A (prompted reflection, no fine-tuning): F1 0.761, JBB DR 89.7% -- indistinguishable from clean baseline
Ablation Condition B (SFT on verdict labels only, no reflection text): F1 0.860, JBB DR 100.0%, Adv-Rec 0.845
Ablation Condition C (blind reflections, no GT label during synthesis): F1 0.836, JBB DR 98.9%, Adv-Rec 0.848
Ablation Condition D (full Reflect-Guard): F1 0.842, F2 0.855, JBB DR 98.2%, Adv-Rec 0.921

Training cost: 1,000 examples, ~42M trainable LoRA parameters (0.5% of 8B model), ~1 hour on a single NVIDIA A5000 GPU; LoRA adapter adds ~42MB storage; inference overhead ~50-100 additional reflection tokens
baselines_compared: Llama-Guard-3-8B with identical 4-bit NF4 quantization but no LoRA adapter and no reflection prompt (Condition 0 / 'clean baseline' -- the paper's primary baseline throughout)
Condition A: Llama-Guard-3-8B prompted to produce reflections without any fine-tuning (isolates prompting-only effect)
Condition B: QLoRA fine-tuned on verdict labels only, no reflection text in training targets (isolates SFT decision-boundary effect)
Condition C: QLoRA fine-tuned with reflections generated by GPT-4o-mini without access to ground-truth labels (isolates GT-label contribution to teacher quality)
